topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crimson ink of the 'D-' swam before my eyes, mocking the hours I’d poured into that essay. I traced the letter with a trembling finger, the cheap paper feeling suddenly abrasive beneath my touch. My chest tightened, a cold, heavy pressure settling low in my stomach. It wasn't just the mark; it was the implication—that all my effort, all the late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee and sheer stubbornness, amounted to nothing. I pushed the textbook away, the sound a sharp crack in the otherwise silent room. I stood up too quickly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, and walked toward the window, staring out at the indifferent sprawl of the campus, wishing I could just disappear into the grey concrete.

[story 2]

"A C minus? Seriously?" I managed to choke out, the sound thin and reedy. Professor Albright’s voice, calm and measured, echoed in my ears, completely devoid of the recognition of the struggle I’d endured. I clenched my jaw so tightly my temples throbbed. My hands, resting on the desk, began to shake uncontrollably, a subtle tremor that betrayed the internal upheaval. I picked up the graded paper, the edges feeling brittle under my thumb. Every word of feedback seemed to twist into a condemnation. I didn't look up; instead, I focused intensely on a tiny smudge on the corner of the paper, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying sense of deflation.

[story 3]

I reread the feedback three times, slowly, deliberately. Each sentence landed like a small stone in my chest, creating a dull, persistent ache. The air in the study lounge felt suddenly too thick, suffocating. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap vinyl groaning in protest, and let out a breath that felt ragged and shallow. My gaze drifted around the room, cataloging the other students who seemed so effortlessly capable, so composed. A slow, deliberate movement took over: I gathered my notes, shoving them haphazardly into my backpack, the action jerky and uncoordinated. When I finally stood to leave, my shoulders slumped forward, the weight of the disappointing result pulling me down into a heavy, unresponsive slump.
